channel.title: Capacity-Planning on WFM Labs Knowledge Wiki
channel.description: Recent content in Capacity-Planning on WFM Labs Knowledge Wiki

item.title: Annual Attrition
item.description:
Annual attrition is calculated as:

Attrition rate = (Number of employee separations / average number of employees) x 100.

item.title: Annual Salary
item.description: The annual cost associated with an employee’s pay and benefits. For calculating total impact of attrition, we recommend leveraging a loaded salary figure which includes the cost of benefits. If your loaded annual salary incorporates costs associated with IT, HR and other support organizations, ensure you do not double count those in Onboarding Costs .

item.title: Demand calculation
item.description:
A simple demand calculation leveraged across the industry to generate an approximate base FTE required is as follows:

Base FTE Required = Call Volume Offered X Average Handle Time / 3600 / Total Annual Work Hours / Occupancy / (1 - Shrinkage)

This calculation can be leveraged to establish a base FTE needed to service an annual workload in order to begin calculating the true costs associated with employee attrition, as described in the High Cost of Attrition

item.title: Dynamic Calculators
item.description:
Dynamic Calculators, Visual Tools and Model Development

Organizations require effective layouts of information to understand results, discover patterns, tabulate findings, and develop strategies based on this data. WFM Labs seeks to provide a variety of models, reports, and visual analysis tools utilizing various applications to help businesses uncover information.

Shiny & R Studio

[300px|thumb|right|Power of One - Small Queues](image:pow-small.jpg “wikilink”)

R Studio and Shinyapps allow dynamic visualization and working models, exposing them to an online interface. In this example, we’ve created an online visualization of the “ Power of One ” - showing the importance of how losing (or gaining) a single call center rep can dramatically impact interval service level.

item.title: Length of Training
item.description: Length of training is the total time from beginning training class to an employee going into production. It is up to individual organizations and the calculation being leverages as to whether to include nesting periods as part of training length, or as part of production.

item.title: Onboarding Costs
item.description:
Onboarding costs are an important factor to consider when calculating the full financial impact that attrition has on an organization. While some expenses may be hard dollars (example, paying an external recruiting firm), other expenses may be though of as soft dollars, such as employees time spent supporting IT and HR requirements.

In considering the full cost of attrition to an organization, WFM Labs recommends establishing an average cost per agent to capture both hard and soft dollar expenses, looking minimally at:

item.title: Power of One
item.description:
Achieving Service Level

WFM teams strive to meet service level goals throughout every interval of the day, even when unexpected situations arise. Automation is critical for intraday management tactics to mitigate unforeseen events such as frontline staff calling in sick, weather events, sudden technical troubles, or unexpectedly high call volumes. While WFM teams take action to make adjustments to maintaining service levels in the face of these events, WFM teams can not respond quickly enough alone, without automation, to maximize opportunities to optimize service levels when variance is introduced.

item.title: Speed to proficiency curve
item.description:
The speed to proficiency curve expresses a relationship between the Average Handle Time for an employee when they begin taking calls against the months involved before they become “proficient” - or able to handle calls at the same efficiency as an employee who is said to be tenured.

In the simplest method, we can plot that in a linear relationship on two axis, where X represents the months required to become proficient, and Y represents the average handle time:

item.title: Training Attrition
item.description:
Training attrition references the % of people who leave at some point after starting training, but prior to entering full production. The calculation for training attrition is:

Training Attrition % = (Number of new hires who exit / Total New Hires) X 100

Training attrition can be calculated at an individual class level, by instructor, by business segment or across an entire organization.
